title: Lord of the Rings The Fellowship of the Ring
author: J R R Tolkien
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "Lord of the Rings The Fellowship of the Ring" book by J R R Tolkien?
Options:
A. The hills got closer as the hobbits traveled on. The hills rose and fell, forming a wavy ridge that was sometimes nearly a thousand feet high, with low passes that led further east. On top of the ridge, the hobbits saw what seemed to be old, overgrown walls and barriers made of stone. In the passes, there were ruins of ancient stone structures. By nighttime they had reached the bottom of the westward side of the hills, and they set up camp there. It was October 5th, and they had been traveling for six days since leaving Bree.
B. The hills were drawing closer as the hobbits went along. They formed an up-and-down ridge, which frequently rose to nearly one thousand feet high, but also dropped down into low passes that led into the country eastward. Along the ridgetop, the hobbits saw what looked to be old remains of green stone walls and barriers. In the passes, the ruins of ancient stone structures still stood. By nighttime they had reached the bottom of the western slopes, and they set up camp there. It was October 5th, and they had been traveling for six days since departing from Bree.
C. The hills drew nearer. They made an undulating ridge, often rising almost to a thousand feet, and here and there falling again to low clefts or passes leading into the eastern land beyond. Along the crest of the ridge the hobbits could see what looked to be the remains of green-grown walls and dikes, and in the clefts there still stood the ruins of old works of stone. By night they had reached the feet of the westward slopes, and there they camped. It was the night of the fifth of October, and they were six days out from Bree.
D. As the hobbits continued on, the hills came nearer. The hills made a rolling ridge, often climbing to heights of almost one thousand feet, but also dropping down into low gaps that provided entry into the lands to the east. Along the top of the ridge, the hobbits glimpsed what appeared to be remnants of green walls and dikes made of stone. In the gaps, the ruins of old stoneworks still stood. By nightfall they had come to the feet of the west-facing slopes, and there they made camp. It was the night of October 5th, and six days had passed since they left Bree.
Answer:

options.A: The hills got closer as the hobbits traveled on. The hills rose and fell, forming a wavy ridge that was sometimes nearly a thousand feet high, with low passes that led further east. On top of the ridge, the hobbits saw what seemed to be old, overgrown walls and barriers made of stone. In the passes, there were ruins of ancient stone structures. By nighttime they had reached the bottom of the westward side of the hills, and they set up camp there. It was October 5th, and they had been traveling for six days since leaving Bree.
options.B: The hills were drawing closer as the hobbits went along. They formed an up-and-down ridge, which frequently rose to nearly one thousand feet high, but also dropped down into low passes that led into the country eastward. Along the ridgetop, the hobbits saw what looked to be old remains of green stone walls and barriers. In the passes, the ruins of ancient stone structures still stood. By nighttime they had reached the bottom of the western slopes, and they set up camp there. It was October 5th, and they had been traveling for six days since departing from Bree.

Along the crest of the ridge the hobbits could see what looked to be the remains of green-grown walls and dikes, and in the clefts there still stood the ruins of old works of stone. By night they had reached the feet of the westward slopes, and there they camped. It was the night of the fifth of October, and they were six days out from Bree.
options.D: As the hobbits continued on, the hills came nearer. The hills made a rolling ridge, often climbing to heights of almost one thousand feet, but also dropping down into low gaps that provided entry into the lands to the east. Along the top of the ridge, the hobbits glimpsed what appeared to be remnants of green walls and dikes made of stone. In the gaps, the ruins of old stoneworks still stood. By nightfall they had come to the feet of the west-facing slopes, and there they made camp. It was the night of October 5th, and six days had passed since they left Bree.
